content.assessment: No supplied international case directly tests a coaching-resistant, SES-weighted admission redesign that preserves early tracking structure. The comparative evidence base here concerns tracking's structural effects (F-live-international_comparison-01/02/03/04) and Hungary's distinctive tracking-choice interaction (F-live-international_comparison-09), not exam-design interventions. The Bavarian micro-evidence on poor predictive validity of age-10 selection (F-live-international_comparison-05, weak) is suggestive that selection instruments generally perform poorly at sorting ability regardless of design, which bears indirectly on whether a redesigned exam can achieve fair sorting. Because Hungary's segregation is bundled with unrestricted free choice (F-live-international_comparison-09), an admissions-only fix addresses a narrower slice of the mechanism than comparators suggest is necessary.
opportunities: Could be evaluated against Bavarian-style micro-validity studies to test whether coaching-resistant items actually reduce SES-driven mis-sorting
strengths: Targets the exam instrument, which the weak Bavarian evidence suggests already has questionable predictive validity for selection generally, implying reform of the instrument is a plausible lever independent of country context
threats: Cross-national evidence on tracking (F-live-international_comparison-01/02/03) suggests the between-school inequality problem in Hungary is structural and severe; an admissions-only fix may leave the larger structural driver untouched, risking a mismatch between the scale of the intervention and the scale of the documented problem
weaknesses: No cross-national precedent supplied for exam-redesign-only interventions leaving tracking structure intact; comparability to Finnish/Polish bundled reforms is low

Hungary's segregation pattern is described as arising from tracking interacting with free choice (F-live-international_comparison-09), a bundled mechanism the proposal does not address, limiting transferability of any single-lever fix
